title: Predecessors
tex_block_html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $<$ a total order of $J \cup V$. The set of predecessors of $v$ in $G$ are: \[\Pred^G_<(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC.\] We also put: \[\Pred^G_\le(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.\]

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_9 -- "Predecessors".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_9
% title: Predecessors
\def\rowref{def\_3\_9}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_9}

\begin{Def}[Predecessors]
    \label{def-predecessors}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg}. Let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$, read as a \emph{strict} total order in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order} (irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on $J \cup V$); the symbol $<$ is read strictly throughout. The element $v$ is left unconstrained by the LN -- in particular, the LN does \emph{not} impose $v \in J \cup V$ -- and we follow that literal stance below; note that the comparison $w < v$ is only directly given by $<$ when $v \in J \cup V$, since $<$ is supplied only as a total order of $J \cup V$. Adopting the convention of def \ref{not-cdmg}, item~1, that ``$w \in G$'' abbreviates ``$w \in J \cup V$'', we write the set-builder bodies below explicitly with $w \in J \cup V$.

    The set of \emph{predecessors} of $v$ in $G$ is
    \[
        \Pred^G_<(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC.
    \]
    We also put
    \[
        \Pred^G_\le(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.
    \]

    \emph{Notational quirk: the ``$\le$'' subscript vs.\ the strict body.} The subscript ``$\le$'' on $\Pred^G_\le$ marks the non-strict variant, but the set-builder body the LN writes is \emph{strict} (the membership condition is $w < v$, not $w \le v$), with the singleton $\{v\}$ adjoined separately. We implement this literal LN body $\Pred^G_<(v) \cup \{v\}$, \emph{not} the alternative $\lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w \le v \rC$; the two forms coincide whenever $v \in J \cup V$ (irreflexivity of $<$ keeps $v$ out of the strict body, while the reflexive-closure form $w \le v$ would pick it up via $v \le v$), and they diverge only in the corner case noted next.

    \emph{Corner case $v \notin J \cup V$.} Under the literal LN body, $w < v$ for $w \in J \cup V$ is not provided by the total order $<$ (whose domain is $J \cup V$), so the natural reading is $\Pred^G_<(v) = \emptyset$; the non-strict body's adjoined singleton then degenerately gives $\Pred^G_\le(v) = \{v\}$, so $v$ is its own non-strict predecessor even when $v$ lies outside the underlying node set of $G$. We follow the LN's lack of hypothesis on $v$ and do \emph{not} impose $v \in J \cup V$ here; downstream consumers that pattern-match on the shape of an element of $\Pred^G_<(v)$ or $\Pred^G_\le(v)$ may assume $v \in J \cup V$ at the point of use as a separate hypothesis.
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg. Let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$, read as a strict total order in the sense of def def-topological-order (irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on $J \cup V$); the symbol $<$ is read strictly throughout. The element $v$ is left unconstrained by the LN ‚Äď in particular, the LN does not impose $v \in J \cup V$ ‚Äď and we follow that literal stance below; note that the comparison $w < v$ is only directly given by $<$ when $v \in J \cup V$, since $<$ is supplied only as a total order of $J \cup V$. Adopting the convention of def not-cdmg, item 1, that ‚Äú$w \in G$‚ÄĚ abbreviates ‚Äú$w \in J \cup V$‚ÄĚ, we write the set-builder bodies below explicitly with $w \in J \cup V$.

The set of predecessors of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Pred^G_<(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC. \] We also put \[ \Pred^G_\le(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}. \]

Notational quirk: the ‚Äú$\le$‚ÄĚ subscript vs.\ the strict body. The subscript ‚Äú$\le$‚ÄĚ on $\Pred^G_\le$ marks the non-strict variant, but the set-builder body the LN writes is strict (the membership condition is $w < v$, not $w \le v$), with the singleton $\{v\}$ adjoined separately. We implement this literal LN body $\Pred^G_<(v) \cup \{v\}$, not the alternative $\lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w \le v \rC$; the two forms coincide whenever $v \in J \cup V$ (irreflexivity of $<$ keeps $v$ out of the strict body, while the reflexive-closure form $w \le v$ would pick it up via $v \le v$), and they diverge only in the corner case noted next.

Corner case $v \notin J \cup V$. Under the literal LN body, $w < v$ for $w \in J \cup V$ is not provided by the total order $<$ (whose domain is $J \cup V$), so the natural reading is $\Pred^G_<(v) = \emptyset$; the non-strict body's adjoined singleton then degenerately gives $\Pred^G_\le(v) = \{v\}$, so $v$ is its own non-strict predecessor even when $v$ lies outside the underlying node set of $G$. We follow the LN's lack of hypothesis on $v$ and do not impose $v \in J \cup V$ here; downstream consumers that pattern-match on the shape of an element of $\Pred^G_<(v)$ or $\Pred^G_\le(v)$ may assume $v \in J \cup V$ at the point of use as a separate hypothesis.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `Pred` and `PredLE`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for this row in particular: the set-builder body `{w | w ‚ąą G ‚ąß lt w v}` invokes `w ‚ąą G`, which via the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2` reduces to `Finset` membership on `G.J ‚ą™ G.V` ‚ÄĒ and `Finset` operations require decidable equality on the element type. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not needed here and is left to downstream use sites that actually consume it.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `w ‚ąą G` via Finset membership.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
def Pred (G : CDMG Node) (lt : Node ‚Üí Node ‚Üí Prop) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w ‚ąą G ‚ąß lt w v}
lean_blocks.explanation: This block defines the strict-predecessor operator `Pred`, taking three arguments ‚ÄĒ a CDMG `G`, an external order relation `lt : Node ‚Üí Node ‚Üí Prop`, and a node `v` ‚ÄĒ and returning a `Set Node`. The body is a set-builder collecting every node `w` that both lies in the graph (`w ‚ąą G`, which via `def_3_2`'s `Membership` instance unfolds to `w ‚ąą G.J ‚ą™ G.V`) and is ordered before `v` under `lt`. A crucial design point: `lt` is taken as raw data rather than pinned to a canonical `[LT Node]` typeclass, so downstream consumers (d-/ŌÉ-separation, do-calculus) can plug in whichever order they want ‚ÄĒ typically a topological order produced by `claim_3_2`. The return type is `Set Node` rather than `Finset Node`: this avoids demanding `Decidable (lt w v)` at the definitional level, with finiteness recoverable later from `G.J ‚ą™ G.V` being a `Finset`. Namespacing under `CDMG` parallels the `Pa`/`Ch` family-set conventions of `def_3_5`, so call sites read `G.Pred lt v`.

lean_blocks.code:
def PredLE (G : CDMG Node) (lt : Node ‚Üí Node ‚Üí Prop) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  G.Pred lt v ‚ą™ {v}
lean_blocks.explanation: This block defines the non-strict variant `PredLE`, obtained from the strict `Pred` by adjoining `v` itself. The `LE` suffix flags the non-strict version, paralleling Mathlib's `<`/`‚Č§` naming distinction. The body `G.Pred lt v ‚ą™ {v}` uses dot-notation through the `CDMG` namespace ‚ÄĒ `G.Pred lt v` desugars to `CDMG.Pred G lt v` ‚ÄĒ together with Mathlib's `Set.union` operator `‚ą™` and the singleton `{v}` (which Lean elaborates to `Set.singleton v` because the left-hand side of `‚ą™` is a `Set Node`). An equivalent spelling would be `insert v (G.Pred lt v)`, but `‚ą™ {v}` reads most directly as the LN's "predecessors plus the node itself". A subtle consequence of taking the literal LN spelling: `v ‚ąą G.PredLE lt v` holds unconditionally, even when `v ‚ąČ G`, since the adjoined singleton carries no membership guard. Downstream "ancestors-plus-self" lemmas can now invoke `PredLE` without case-splitting on `w = v ‚ą® lt w v`.

design_choices:
**Typing context `Node : Type*` with `[DecidableEq Node]`, inherited verbatim from `def_3_1`.** Both fixtures are load-bearing for the set-builder body `{w | w ‚ąą G ‚ąß lt w v}`. The conjunct `w ‚ąą G` is not custom notation: it routes through the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2` (`CDMGNotation.lean`), which unfolds `w ‚ąą G` to `w ‚ąą G.J ‚ą™ G.V` ‚ÄĒ a `Finset` membership check that reduces to decidable equality on `Node`. Dropping `[DecidableEq Node]` would break the `Finset.mem` reduction and the body would fail to elaborate. Stronger instances (`Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) are deliberately *not* picked up here and are deferred to consumers who need them. The `variable` line is gated with the three-dash `--- start helper` marker (rather than the two-dash `-- start statement` reserved for formalised LN content), matching the convention across `CDMG.lean`, `TopologicalOrder.lean`, and friends ‚ÄĒ it binds the implicit parameters the two `def`s below rely on but is not itself part of the LN definition.

**The strict order is passed as an explicit `lt : Node ‚Üí Node ‚Üí Prop` parameter, not via a `[LT Node]` typeclass, a bundled structure field, or an `‚ąÉ lt, ‚Ä¶` existential.** The LN reads "let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$" and then writes $\operatorname{Pred}^G_<(v)$ ‚ÄĒ the subscript $<$ is a *parameter* the predecessor set is computed against, not a structural feature of nodes. Mirroring the parameter convention of `def_3_8`'s `IsTopologicalOrder`, `Pred G lt v` reads on the nose as the LN's "predecessors of $v$ in $G$ under $<$". A `[LT Node]` instance would glue a single canonical strict order to the *type* `Node`, but a single `Node` type underlies every CDMG and the theory admits many topological orders per graph. Threading the witness from `claim_3_2` (existence of *a* topological order on an acyclic CDMG) would conflate this row's predecessor-set computation with the downstream existence statement, and would prevent consumers from feeding in non-topological `lt` for ad hoc constructions. `Pred` and `PredLE` are *predicate-style operators* that accept any strict relation; the d-separation and ŌÉ-separation machinery in later chapters typically supplies an `lt` carrying a `G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` hypothesis, but the definitional shape does not require it.

**Two top-level defs ‚ÄĒ strict `Pred` and non-strict `PredLE` ‚ÄĒ rather than a single operator parameterised on `<`/`‚Č§` or `PredLE` as a thin notation wrapper.** The LN explicitly introduces *both* $\operatorname{Pred}^G_<$ and $\operatorname{Pred}^G_\le$, and both names recur under that distinction in later chapters: CBN factorisation and do-calculus reach for the strict form, while iSCM recursion ("values determined up to and including $v$") reaches for the non-strict one. Folding the choice into `def Pred (G) (lt) (strict : Bool) (v)` would force every call site to thread a `Bool` and peel off a `match` before reasoning about set membership. A pure `notation` abbreviation would leave `PredLE` un-`@[simp]`-able as its own head symbol. Both downsides are avoided by exposing two named `def`s; the second is a one-line wrapper `G.Pred lt v ‚ą™ {v}`, so the duplication cost is negligible.

**Output type `Set Node`, not `Finset Node` or the subtype `‚Ü•(G.J ‚ą™ G.V)`.** The chapter's family-set primitives (`Pa`, `Ch`, `Sib`, `Anc`, `Desc`, `Sc`, `Dist` from `def_3_5`) all live in `Set Node`, and downstream uses freely intersect predecessor sets with family sets (e.g.¬†$\operatorname{Pred}^G_<(v) \cap \operatorname{Pa}^G(v)$ in do-calculus, $\operatorname{Pred}^G_<(v) \cap V$ in CBN factorisation). A `Finset Node` carrier would thread a `Decidable (lt w v)` instance through every call site ‚ÄĒ the LN never picks a decidable representative for $<$ ‚ÄĒ and would force `Finset.coe` round-trips against the ambient family sets. The subtype carrier would force `Subtype.val` coercions throughout and would make `PredLE`'s adjoined `{v}` ill-typed in the corner case $v \notin J \cup V$. The LN-promised finiteness is still recoverable downstream ‚ÄĒ the underlying carrier `G.J ‚ą™ G.V` *is* a `Finset` with decidable membership ‚ÄĒ but is not exposed at the API surface here; a `Finset`-flavoured parallel lemma can be derived later if needed.

**The `w ‚ąą G` conjunct lives inside the set-builder body, not as a downstream intersection.** The LN's literal $\{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\}$ bounds the first conjunct on $w \in J \cup V$ rather than letting $w$ range over arbitrary `Node`. Two payoffs: (i) under `def_3_2`'s `Membership` instance, `w ‚ąą G` unfolds transparently to `w ‚ąą G.J ‚ą™ G.V`, preserving the literal LN grep correspondence with no convention to smuggle in; (ii) destructuring `h : w ‚ąą Pred G lt v` as `‚ü®hw_mem, hw_lt‚ü©` immediately delivers `hw_mem : w ‚ąą G` without a separate intersection step, matching the precedent set by `Pa`, `Ch`, `Sib` in `FamilyRelationships.lean`. If `lt` happens to be well-typed (e.g.¬†the strict part of a topological order on `G`, whose domain is `J ‚ą™ V`), then `lt w v` already only fires for `w ‚ąą G` and the conjunct is redundant ‚ÄĒ but the definition does *not* assume well-typedness, and the explicit `w ‚ąą G` makes the API robust to arbitrary `lt`.

**`PredLE`'s body is the literal LN spelling `G.Pred lt v ‚ą™ {v}`, not the reflexive-closure form `{w | w ‚ąą G ‚ąß (lt w v ‚ą® w = v)}` or `{w | w ‚Č§ v}`.** The LN subscript reads $\le$ but the body it actually writes is *strict* ‚ÄĒ $\{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\} \cup \{v\}$, with the singleton adjoined unconditionally. The two forms agree whenever $v \in J \cup V$ (irreflexivity of $<$ keeps $v$ out of the strict body, while $w \le v$ would pick it up via $v \le v$) and diverge only in the corner case $v \notin J \cup V$: under the literal LN body the adjoined `{v}` carries no `v ‚ąą G` guard, so `v ‚ąą PredLE G lt v` *unconditionally*. The body is spelled with the named `G.Pred` rather than unfolded ‚ÄĒ partly because it reads on the nose as "the strict predecessor set plus $v$", and partly because downstream proofs holding `h : w ‚ąą G.Pred lt v` can apply `Or.inl h` to obtain `w ‚ąą G.PredLE lt v` with no intermediate `Set.mem_setOf` unfolding. The union is Mathlib's `Set.union` / `‚ą™` notation; the singleton `{v}` resolves to `Set.instSingleton` because the left operand of `‚ą™` is `Set Node`, so no ascription is needed. `‚ą™ {v}` is preferred over `insert v (G.Pred lt v)` ‚ÄĒ both are valid Mathlib idioms, but the union form reads most directly as the LN's "predecessors plus the node itself", and the standard destructors `Set.mem_union` and `Set.mem_singleton_iff` handle membership cleanly. Naming-wise, `Pred` and `PredLE` are namespaced under `CDMG` (enabling `G.Pred lt v`), use Mathlib `PascalCase` for set-valued operators paralleling `Pa` / `PaSet` from `def_3_5`, and the `LE` suffix signals the non-strict variant while keeping the strict form's name short.
